name: Puerto Madrona y Puerto de los Rehoyos — circular por el Pantano del Montoro
region_intro: Road cycling routes around San Lorenzo de Calatrava traverse the diverse landscapes of the Sierra Morena, including the Valle de Alcudia and Sierra Madrona Natural Park. The region features a combination of mountains, holm oak forests, and Mediterranean woodlands, providing varied topography for cyclists. Routes often include significant elevation changes, with valleys carved by rivers such as the Rigüelo and the Grande. This terrain offers challenging climbs and scenic vistas for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around San Lorenzo De Calatrava The most popular road cycling route is Puerto Madrona y Puerto de los Rehoyos — circular por el Pantano del Montoro, a 68.4 miles (110.0 km) trail that takes 6 hours 4 minutes to complete. This route features substantial elevation gain and panoramic views of the Pantano del Montoro. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Fresnedas River Gorges and Collado Pisa Loop, a difficult 50.1 miles (80.7 km) path. This route winds through river gorges and includes the notable Collado Pisa climb, offering varied scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Sierra Morena and Collado Pisa Loop, a 88.3 miles (142.1 km) trail leading through the Sierra Morena mountains, often completed in about 6 hours 51 minutes. Road cycling around San Lorenzo De Calatrava is defined by mountains, holm oak forests, and Mediterranean woodlands. The network offers options for different ability levels, with all listed routes being difficult. More than 90 road cyclists have used komoot to explore San Lorenzo De Calatrava's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around San Lorenzo de Calatrava?
A: Road cycling routes in San Lorenzo de Calatrava offer a diverse landscape, characterized by the Sierra Morena mountains, extensive holm oak forests, and Mediterranean woodlands. You'll encounter varied topography, from challenging mountain slopes to valleys carved by rivers like the Rigüelo and the Grande, providing both demanding climbs and scenic vistas.

Q: Are there challenging climbs for experienced road cyclists in the region?
A: Yes, San Lorenzo de Calatrava is known for its challenging climbs. Notable ascents include the climb to El Centenillo, which spans 5.36 km with an average gradient of 6.8% and a positive elevation of 369 meters. Another significant climb is the Collado Pisa from Huertezuelas, gaining nearly 300 meters over 5 km with an average gradient of 5.6%, rewarding cyclists with incredible views. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249475549/puerto-madrona-y-puerto-de-los-rehoyos-circular-por-el-pantano-del-montoro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Madrona y Puerto de los Rehoyos — circular por el Pantano del Montoro&lt;/a&gt; feature substantial elevation gain.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in San Lorenzo de Calatrava?
A: The region's Mediterranean climate generally makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and lush landscapes. Summer can be quite hot, especially at midday, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended during those months. Winters are typically mild, though some higher elevation routes might be cooler.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The region boasts numerous panoramic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5314619" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo Nuevo Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; offers sweeping vistas of the Rio Grande Valley and the Sierra de El Puntal. Many routes, especially those traversing the Sierra Morena, provide stunning natural backdrops of mountains and forests.

Q: What historical or cultural attractions can I explore while road cycling?
A: You can integrate several historical and cultural points of interest into your rides. The abandoned mining center and historic ruins at &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5323968" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Centenillo&lt;/a&gt; make for a fascinating mid-ride stop. In San Lorenzo de Calatrava itself, you can see the San Lorenzo Mártir church, the old washing place, and traditional circular threshing floors. The La Jalbegada cave paintings, a Site of Cultural Interest, are also nearby.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1273156311/fresnedas-river-gorges-and-collado-pisa-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fresnedas River Gorges and Collado Pisa Loop&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1084914119/sierra-morena-and-collado-pisa-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Morena and Collado Pisa Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offer varied scenery and challenging terrain.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in San Lorenzo de Calatrava?
A: Parking is generally available in and around the village of San Lorenzo de Calatrava. For routes starting further afield, you can often find suitable parking in smaller villages or designated areas near trailheads. It's advisable to check specific starting points for parking availability when planning your route.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in the area?
A: The road cycling routes around San Lorenzo de Calatrava are predominantly classified as difficult due to significant elevation changes and distances. While there are no specifically designated 'easy' road cycling routes in this guide, families with older, experienced cycling members might find some segments manageable. However, for younger children or beginners, these routes may be too challenging.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay in San Lorenzo de Calatrava?
A: San Lorenzo de Calatrava is a small village, but it offers basic amenities. You can find local establishments for refreshments and traditional Spanish cuisine. For accommodation, there might be guesthouses or rural lodgings available, providing a tranquil base for your cycling adventures. It's recommended to check availability in advance, especially during peak seasons.

Q: What wildlife might I encounter while cycling in the Sierra Morena?
A: The Sierra Morena, including the Valle de Alcudia and Sierra Madrona Natural Park, is a critical habitat for significant wildlife. Cyclists might have the chance to spot species such as the Iberian Imperial Eagle, Iberian Lynx, Black Vulture, and Black Stork, adding an ecological dimension to your outdoor exploration.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in this guide for San Lorenzo de Calatrava?
A: This guide features 6 road cycling routes around San Lorenzo de Calatrava, all of which are classified as difficult, offering challenging and rewarding experiences for road cyclists.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by bodies of water or reservoirs?
A: Yes, some routes offer views of local water features. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249475549/puerto-madrona-y-puerto-de-los-rehoyos-circular-por-el-pantano-del-montoro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Madrona y Puerto de los Rehoyos — circular por el Pantano del Montoro&lt;/a&gt; route provides panoramic views of the Pantano del Montoro reservoir, adding a beautiful element to the ride.

Puerto de los Rehoyos is a mountain pass located in Solana del Pino, in the province of Ciudad Real, Castilla-La Mancha. With an altitude of 986 metres, it offers magnificent views of the Sierra de la Solana and the Alcudia Valley. It is a popular place for hiking and cycling, with routes that allow you to enjoy the local fauna, such as deer and birds of prey.

The CR-500 road, which passes through the pass, is in good condition and is ideal for those who enjoy outdoor activities.
Cycling along the CR-501 road bordering the Pantano del Montoro. The concrete dam that contains the waters of the Montoro River is impressive.
